title: Lecture 4: The Resurrection of the Righteous and the Unrighteous
speaker: Pastor Steve Brinegar
series: Runtabers Lecture Series on Historic Pre-Millennialism
source_files: Lecture 4_ The Resurrection of the Righteous and the Unrighteous.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Setting and Approach
subpoints: Pastor Steve Brindiger, president of the board of directors of Western Reformed Seminary and graduate of that institution, delivers the fourth and final lecture from his study at Grace Bible Presbyterian Church in Florida
It has been an honor to be included alongside three men who were his seminary professors
The lecture concerns the fact that there are two resurrections separated by a period of time — the first resurrection (of the righteous) and the second (of the unrighteous)
Points 1 and 2 (resurrection as foundational and resurrection as of several types) are common to all biblical Christianity; point 3 (two distinct phases) is distinctive to pre-millennialism
Grace is urged for those who disagree on eschatological matters: we have far more in common than we disagree on

outline.title: Point 1: Resurrection Is a Foundational Biblical Truth
subpoints: Resurrection was an early doctrine of the people of God: Job 19:25-26 (likely contemporary with Abraham) — 'in my flesh I shall see God'
Paul in Philippians 3:10-21: the resurrection motivates all his service; 'that by any means I may attain the resurrection from the dead'
1 Corinthians 15: without the resurrection, Christian faith is futile, preaching is vain, and we are 'of all people most to be pitied'
Jesus in John 11:25: 'I am the resurrection and the life' — he defines his ministry in resurrection terms
Matthew 22:23-33: Jesus rebukes the Sadducees for knowing neither the Scriptures nor the power of God regarding resurrection

Resurrection is the unifying story from Genesis to Revelation: God created life, man's sin brought death (Genesis 3), Jesus restored life (1 Corinthians 15:21-22), and the tree of life reappears in Revelation 22:2

outline.title: Point 2: Resurrection Is of Several Types
subpoints: Category 1 — Spiritual/covenantal resurrection: death in Adam and life in Christ (Romans 5:12-22; 1 Corinthians 15:21-22); this is regeneration and justification, our covenantal resurrection in Christ

Category 2 — Temporary resurrection: the raising of Lazarus, Tabitha, and others; these persons presumably died again since they fall out of the biblical narrative; the Bible never presents them as models of the final resurrection
Jesus' words in Luke 16 apply: the living do not need someone raised from the dead to tell them what lies beyond — Moses and the prophets are sufficient

Category 3 — Final or eternal resurrection: this is of two types — the resurrection of life (to everlasting joy) and the resurrection of judgment (to everlasting contempt); Daniel 12:2; Matthew 25:31-46; John 5:28-29
The resurrection of the just is based on what we have in Christ; the resurrection of judgment flows from those who have rejected him

outline.title: Point 3: The Final Resurrection Happens in Two Distinct Phases
subpoints: The hallmark of pre-millennialism: the final resurrection of life and the final resurrection of judgment are separated by a period — the millennium

Revelation 20:1-5 is the battleground: Satan is bound for a thousand years; souls who died for Christ 'came to life' and reigned with Christ; 'the rest of the dead did not come to life until the thousand years were ended'

The binding of Satan: the image of an angel chaining Satan in the abyss does not square with 1 Peter 5:8 ('your adversary the devil walks about as a roaring lion') — therefore this binding is a future event, not the present age

Three options for 'the first resurrection' in Revelation 20:4-5: (a) presence in paradise with Christ; (b) the covenantal/spiritual resurrection (salvation); (c) the bodily resurrection of life before the millennium

Why option (c) is correct: the souls are already pictured as present before they 'came to life,' ruling out option (a); their death was martyrdom, not Adamic rebellion, ruling out option (b); the parallel with 'the rest of the dead did not come to life' demands a physical resurrection in both instances

outline.title: Revelation 20 and the Great White Throne Judgment
subpoints: The final rebellion (Gog and Magog, verses 7-10): all the wicked nations surround the saints and the beloved city; fire comes from heaven and destroys them — the saints do not even lift a sword
Application: no difficulty we face compares to the sum of humanity's strength; yet God destroys all his enemies in a moment — our trials are purposeful and temporary

Revelation 20:11-14 (the great white throne): the dead — great and small — stand before the throne; books are opened and the dead are judged according to their works; the book of life determines eternal destiny
Death and Hades are thrown into the lake of fire — the second death; anyone not found in the book of life follows them
The first resurrection people have already been with Christ during the millennium — the second death has no power over them; they are participants in the resurrection of life

outline.title: Conclusion: The Glorious Future and Revelation 21
subpoints: The lecture closes by drawing attention to Revelation 21: the dwelling place of God is with man; God will dwell with his people
Revelation 21:3-4: 'He will wipe away every tear from their eyes. Death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away'

The resurrection is one of the main stories of Scripture, leading us from Genesis (loss of the tree of life) to Revelation (restoration of the tree of life) — a great hope and a great motivation for present service
Paul's conclusion in 1 Corinthians 15: 'Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain'

summary:
Pastor Steve Brindiger, president of the board of directors of Western Reformed Seminary and a graduate of that institution, delivers the fourth and final lecture from his study at Grace Bible Presbyterian Church in Florida. His subject is the resurrection of the dead — specifically the pre-millennial thesis that the final resurrection occurs in two distinct phases: the resurrection of the righteous before the millennium and the resurrection of the unrighteous after it.

The lecture is organized in three points that move from common ground to distinctively pre-millennial territory. Point one establishes resurrection as a foundational biblical truth shared by all biblical Christianity, tracing it from Job (a contemporary of Abraham), through Philippians 3 and 1 Corinthians 15, through Jesus' declaration 'I am the resurrection and the life,' to the grand narrative arc from Genesis 3 (loss of access to the tree of life) to Revelation 22 (the tree of life restored). Point two identifies four types of resurrection in Scripture — the covenantal resurrection of salvation (death in Adam, life in Christ), temporary resurrections (Lazarus and others, who presumably died again), and the final eternal resurrections of life and judgment — and shows that these categories are acknowledged by believers of all eschatological persuasions.

Point three is the pre-millennial contribution. Drawing on Revelation 20:1-14, Pastor Brindiger argues that the 'first resurrection' of verse 4 is the bodily resurrection of believers before the millennium, not their present heavenly rest or their spiritual regeneration. He reasons that the souls of the martyrs are already depicted as present before they 'came to life,' eliminating option (a); and their death was martyrdom for Christ, not Adamic rebellion, eliminating option (b). The binding of Satan — depicted as an angel physically chaining him in the abyss — is argued to be a future event, since it cannot be reconciled with Peter's warning that Satan 'walks about as a roaring lion seeking whom he may devour' in the present age. The lecture concludes by drawing the believer's gaze forward to Revelation 21's vision of God dwelling with his people forever, where death, mourning, and pain are no more — the resurrection as the great motivating hope for present service.
transcript:
## Introduction

In this fourth and last lecture, Pastor Steve Brindiger, the president of our board of directors and a graduate of Western Reformed Seminary, is going to teach us regarding the resurrection of the righteous in the beginning of the millennium and the resurrection of the unrighteous at the end of the millennium.

You've made it to the final lecture. And this final lecture is on a vital doctrine of the Bible. I would like to just say it's been an honor to be included along with three men who taught me when I was in seminary. You might notice that the location has shifted from the Tacoma Bible Presbyterian Church to here in sunny Florida. And we're at my study at the Grace Bible Presbyterian Church.

My topic is that of the resurrection. It's a topic that flows out of the previous topics. You noticed that in Dr. Battle's lecture, how often the idea of resurrection — particularly what I'm going to call the first resurrection or the resurrection of the righteous or the resurrection of life — came up. You also heard it in Professor Lynch's lecture about the resurrection coming before the final battle against Gog and Magog. And that will come up again in this lecture.

My lecture is on the resurrection — particularly on the fact that there are two resurrections separated by a period of time. My approach is going to be: first, resurrection is a foundational biblical truth; second, resurrection is of several types; and third, that final resurrection will happen in two distinct phases.

Now as you hear that outline, let me acknowledge at the very beginning that points one and two are common to biblical Christianity. No matter what your view of eschatology is, you actually have to agree with those first two points. But they help us understand something very important: no matter which of the systems you hold to, we have more in agreement than we disagree. We want to hold these matters with grace toward those who might disagree. The difference comes at my third point, which is a hallmark of pre-millennialism.

## Point 1: Resurrection Is a Foundational Biblical Truth

The reality is that resurrection was a very early doctrine of the people of God. We see that in Job 19:25-26. Job is likely a contemporary of Abraham. Therefore the doctrine that we find in that book — the words that we find in the mouth of Job — are a very early expression, an expression that happens before the Pentateuch is given by Moses. And yet from Adam to Job, and for all time, the people of God have had an understanding about resurrection. Because resurrection is a foundational biblical truth.

Not only do we see it as a very early doctrine, but secondly we see that it's foundational because Paul said so. Turn to Philippians chapter 3, starting in verse 10. The Apostle Paul says, as he speaks about his own testimony: "that I may know him and the power of his resurrection, and may share his sufferings, becoming like him in his death, that by any means possible I may attain the resurrection from the dead. Not that I have already obtained this or am already perfect, but I press on to make it my own, because Christ Jesus has made me his own." Paul, as he's talking about his service of the Lord, is rooted and grounded in the idea of a resurrection. He's motivated and encouraged as he looks forward to what is to come.

Also, notice in verse 21: the possibility of a resurrection is rooted and grounded in the power of God to conquer all things. Resurrection is not something we can prove scientifically. It is received on faith.

Now turn to 1 Corinthians 15. This is just a wondrous chapter about the resurrection, about the glory of God. Starting in verse 10: "But by the grace of God I am what I am, and his grace toward me was not in vain. On the contrary, I worked harder than any of them, though it was not I, but the grace of God that is with me." Then verse 12: "Now if Christ is proclaimed as raised from the dead, how can some of you say that there is no resurrection of the dead? But if there is no resurrection of the dead, then not even Christ has been raised. And if Christ has not been raised, then our preaching is in vain and your faith is in vain... If in Christ we have hope in this life only, we are of all people most to be pitied."

Think about what Paul is saying: our Christian faith is founded upon the resurrection, fundamentally, foundationally on the resurrection of the Lord Jesus Christ. And his bodily resurrection is a resurrection like unto ours — the great hope and blessing of the church.

Jesus himself said it. John 11:25: "I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live, and everyone who lives and believes in me shall never die." Jesus defines his ministry in these resurrection terms.

In Matthew 22:23-33, the Sadducees ask their fancy philosophical question about a woman with seven husbands. Jesus answered: "You are wrong, because you know neither the Scriptures nor the power of God."

**Resurrection as the Unifying Story of Scripture**

Resurrection frames the entire story of Scripture. God created life (Genesis 1-2). Man's sin brought death — Genesis 3:24: God drove out the man and placed a cherub and a flaming sword to guard the way to the tree of life. Man sinned, brought death, and lost access to that life-sustaining tree.

Turn to 1 Corinthians 15:21: "For as by a man came death, by a man has come also the resurrection of the dead. For as in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive. But each in his own order: Christ the firstfruits, then at his coming those who belong to Christ. Then comes the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God the Father after destroying every rule and every authority and power."

Jesus has restored life. And Revelation 22:2 brings it full circle: "also, on either side of the river, the tree of life with its twelve kinds of fruit... the leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations." Here is the restoration of life — the resurrection unto a body like Jesus's glorious body, to be with Jesus for all time.

## Point 2: Resurrection Is of Several Types

There are really four types of resurrection that fit neatly into three categories.

**Category 1: Spiritual/Covenantal Resurrection.** Romans 5:12: "Therefore, just as sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin... death spread to all men because all sinned." In Adam, we all died. But in verse 18: "Therefore, as one trespass led to condemnation for all men, so one act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all men." In Jesus we've been raised. Jesus as our covenant Head succeeded where Adam as our covenant head failed. Jesus is the second Adam. Covenantally, we died in Adam; we've come to life in the Lord Jesus Christ.

**Category 2: Temporary Resurrection.** These are the resurrections we find in passages like John 11 (Lazarus) or Acts 9 (Tabitha). Nowhere in Scripture is the resurrection of Lazarus presented as a model of what we will be like at the final resurrection. Instead, in every case, that person falls out of the biblical narrative. Lazarus did not write a book about "My Three Days in Heaven." Jesus himself said such testimony is completely unnecessary — they have Moses and the prophets (Luke 16). My assumption is that these were temporary resurrections — the person was given their corruptible body back, and they later died. Lazarus is now in perfect peace with Jesus in paradise.

**Category 3: The Final/Eternal Resurrection.** This is of two types:

- The resurrection of life (to everlasting joy)
- The resurrection of judgment (to everlasting contempt)

Daniel 12:2: "Many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt."

Matthew 25:31-33: "Before him will be gathered all the nations, and he will separate people one from another as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats."

John 5:28-29: "Do not marvel at this, for an hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear his voice and come out, those who have done good to the resurrection of life, and those who have done evil to the resurrection of judgment."

Here Jesus says ALL will come forth — some to life, others to judgment. Two types of resurrection. All of Scripture agrees on this. No matter your eschatology, you have to agree that there is a resurrection of life and a resurrection of judgment.

## Point 3: The Final Resurrection Happens in Two Distinct Phases

Now we come to the pre-millennial contribution. My third point is the hallmark of one of the hallmarks of pre-millennialism: that final resurrection — the eternal resurrection — happens in two distinct phases.

We see that in Revelation chapter 20. Turn with me there — the discussion ground within the church.

Let me read verses 1 through 8: "Then I saw an angel coming down from heaven, holding in his hand the key to the bottomless pit and a great chain. And he seized the dragon, that ancient serpent, who is the devil and Satan, and bound him for a thousand years, and threw him into the pit, and shut it and sealed it over him, so that he might not deceive the nations any longer, until the thousand years were ended. After that he must be released for a little while. Then I saw thrones, and seated on them were those to whom the authority to judge was committed. Also I saw the souls of those who had been beheaded for the testimony of Jesus and for the word of God, and those who had not worshiped the beast or its image and had not received its mark on their foreheads or their hands. They came to life and reigned with Christ for a thousand years. The rest of the dead did not come to life until the thousand years were ended. This is the first resurrection. Blessed and holy is the one who shares in the first resurrection! Over such the second death has no power, but they will be priests of God and of Christ, and they will reign with him for a thousand years. And when the thousand years are ended, Satan will be released from his prison and will come out to deceive the nations that are at the four corners of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them for battle; their number is like the sand of the sea."

**Is this first resurrection the resurrection of life?**

Let me say it's a little outside the scope of my lecture, but let me point out something wonderful in the final rebellion (verses 9-10): I don't know what problem you might be facing, but you are not facing the sum total of humanity's strength. And yet here, all the wicked on earth come together, surround the saints of God — and the people of God don't even pick up a sword. Fire comes down from heaven and consumes all the enemies of God's people. Whatever difficulty you face, child of God, there is nothing you face that God could not take from you in a moment if He chose.

Now, the discussion: what is "the first resurrection" in verse 4? There are really three options.

**Option A:** Presence in paradise with Christ — the intermediate state, where the souls of the righteous are conscious with Jesus. But this can't be right: the souls are already depicted as present (the souls of those beheaded are already there) before they "came to life." They're already in paradise before the event described as "came to life."

**Option B:** The covenantal/spiritual resurrection — salvation, regeneration. But this can't be right either: in Adam we don't describe that death as martyrdom. The death described here is martyrdom — being beheaded for the testimony of Jesus. The death in Adam was rebellion. John is not saying that this coming to life is the reversal of Adamic rebellion; he's saying it's the reward of martyrdom for Christ.

**Option C:** The bodily resurrection of life — the first resurrection, the resurrection of the just, before the millennial kingdom. I believe this is the correct reading, for several reasons.

First, the binding of Satan in verses 1-3: an angel has to come down from heaven with a great chain to bind Satan so he cannot deceive the nations. Does that imagery square with 1 Peter 5:8, where Peter writes to the church: "your adversary the devil walks about as a roaring lion, seeking whom he may devour"? Someone who cannot deceive the nations cannot also be a roaring lion seeking to devour God's people. These images do not coexist. Therefore the binding described in Revelation 20 is a future event, not the present age.

Second, the dead in verse 4 died for their faith (martyrdom) and then "came to life" — they were dead and then came to life. Compare: the rest of the dead did not come to life until the end of the thousand years. If the first "came to life" is spiritual or the intermediate state, it makes no sense to describe the second one differently. They are parallel expressions demanding a parallel interpretation: both are physical resurrections, one before the millennium and one after.

Third, this fits with what Professor Lynch described: in Ezekiel 37, the resurrection occurs before the final rebellion of Gog and Magog. In Revelation 20, the saints are living in the city before Gog and Magog make their attack at the end of the millennium. The people of God are already present in bodily form before that final rebellion.

**The Great White Throne Judgment (Revelation 20:11-14)**

After the final rebellion comes the judgment. "And I saw the dead, great and small, standing before the throne, and books were opened. Then another book was opened, which is the book of life. And the dead were judged by what was written in the books, according to what they had done. And the sea gave up the dead who were in it, Death and Hades gave up the dead who were in them, and they were judged, each one of them, according to what they had done. Then Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire. This is the second death, the lake of fire. And if anyone's name was not found written in the book of life, he was thrown into the lake of fire."

The first resurrection people are already with Christ during the millennium — the second death has no power over them (verse 6). Now in this second resurrection, the unjust are raised to judgment. And even here, the book of life is present — meaning that through the millennial kingdom, through the preaching of the gospel, if some who lived on the earth came to believe, they would be found in that book of life. By the power of God, if that's his will, that's what will happen.

## Conclusion: The Glorious Future

I want to close not only my lecture but our lecture series by thinking once again about the glorious future.

Revelation 21:3-4: "And I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, 'Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God. He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away.'"

What a joy it is to think that pain, suffering, death, mourning, crying — all those things are coming to an end. At this last glorious day we will be forever with the Lord.

Truly the resurrection, as one of the main stories of Scripture, leads us to great hope. And as Paul instructs us in 1 Corinthians 15:58: "Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain."

That is the great motivation. That is what the resurrection means for how we live today.
